target.name: Polar Semiconductor
target.founder: Independent spinout of Control Data (2024 reformation)

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Growth → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $700M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 24.4x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Polar Semiconductor is a Growth-stage chip design company and 2024 reformation spinout of Control Data, operating within a sector (Chips & Semiconductors) represented by 3 public comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers including Cerebras and Etched. The company has raised $700M+ to date.

Stage-progression methodology applies here, using total capital raised ($700M) as a proxy for current valuation and applying a typical 1.2x step-up multiplier to reflect advancement within the Growth stage, yielding $840M. Peer-median analysis is not applicable: while 14 same-sector peers exist in the broader universe, filtering for stage, valuation tier, and data density yields insufficient datapoints (fewer than 3 required). Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples—where Semiconductors trade at a 24.4x median across the three public comps—cannot be deployed absent the target's disclosed revenue, which private companies typically withhold.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $714M to $966M, centered on $840M. This single-method conclusion carries material uncertainty, particularly around (1) whether the $700M raised figure appropriately reflects fair value or represents a lower bound, and (2) the absence of revenue visibility that would enable public-multiple triangulation. Investors should treat this range as indicative pending acquisition of additional financial or peer transaction data.
